did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Vision Transformers (ViT) and various CNN architectures (VGG-16, ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, custom CNN), to perform automated skin lesion classification and melanoma detection on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including the development and training of a Vision Transformer model on the HAM10000 dataset, comparison with CNN baselines, and detailed performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score). It is published in the proceedings of the 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Computing Technologies (ICACT).

title: Leveraging Vision Transformers for Automated Skin Lesion Diagnosis: A Case Study on Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Computing Technologies (ICACT)
brief_summary: This study evaluates deep learning models for automated skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset to address the challenges of manual diagnosis. The researchers compared a custom CNN baseline with VGG-16, ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, and a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture. The models were trained and tested using data preprocessing, augmentation, and handling of class imbalance via random over-sampling. The Vision Transformer achieved the highest performance with a maximum accuracy of 99%, outperforming the baseline CNN's 96% accuracy. The ViT demonstrated superior feature representation and high predictive confidence in detecting melanomas, making it particularly promising for clinical use. The results highlight the advantages of transformer-based architectures over traditional convolutional models for dermatological image analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the HAM10000 dataset categories used for multi-class classification.

Highlighted as the most lethal subtype; special emphasis placed on its detection due to critical role in survival rates and reducing false negatives. ViT showed high predictive confidence in detecting melanomas.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin lesion diagnostic categories (multiclass classification of 7 classes including melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automated skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs malignant, or specific lesion types) using a Vision Transformer (ViT) and CNN baselines. The output is determined by a classification head with Softmax activation (y = Softmax(Wc zCLS + bc)). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for multiclass classification. The paper explicitly states it addresses 'multiclass skin cancer classification' and 'skin lesion classification'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset fetched from Kaggle, comprising 10,015 dermatoscopic images from various populations and imaging sources, designed to overcome challenges of limited size and lack of variety in earlier collections.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (ViT, VGG-16, ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, Custom CNN) after splitting into 70% training set.
use.validation: Used for validation after splitting into 15% validation set.
use.testing: Used for testing after splitting into 15% testing set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess predictions and analyze the performance of the ViT model by providing a detailed breakdown of actual versus predicted classifications across lesion classes.

To evaluate the learning behavior, convergence, generalization ability, and signs of overfitting or underfitting of the ViT model over a 50-epoch period by tracking training and validation accuracies and losses.
To handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset during data preprocessing.
To assure repeatability of the experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, along with confusion matrices and training/validation curves.

HAM10000.notes: The ViT achieved the highest performance with a maximum accuracy of 98-99% (text mentions 99% in abstract, 98% in Table II and results section). ViT showed high predictive confidence in detecting melanomas (max probability score of 1.0). The Custom CNN outperformed generic off-the-shelf models (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2) but was outperformed by ViT. VGG16 and ResNet50 showed signs of underfitting and overfitting respectively. MobileNetV2 showed competitive results with lower computational complexity. The model found the ideal balance between fitting training data and preserving validation performance around the 11th epoch. Validation accuracy stabilized at about 98% after 20 epochs.

training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: Linear decay with warmup; Reduce-on-Plateau strategy applied where learning rate was adaptively reduced by a factor α whenever validation accuracy plateaued
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU (32 GB RAM), Intel Xeon Gold CPU, 512 GB RAM
training_details.data_preprocessing: Images reduced to 224x224 pixels, normalized, and dynamically upgraded using affine transformations, random flips, rotations, and brightness adjustments

preprocessing_techniques: Image Path Mapping and Cleaning: Linked dermatoscopic images with metadata using image_id; removed cases with missing photos to preserve dataset integrity.
Label Encoding: Converted diagnostic categories into integer identifiers and expanded into one-hot vectors for model compatibility.
Resizing: Applied a fixed resolution of 224 × 224 pixels to all dermatoscopic images.
Color Correction: Performed BGR to RGB conversion to standardize color channels.
Normalization: Applied pixel intensity normalization to constrain values between 0 and 1 using the formula I_norm(x, y, c) = I(x, y, c) / 255.
Data Augmentation: Incorporated random horizontal flips, small-angle rotations (±10°), zoom variations (±10%), and random shifts in width and height.
Dynamic Upgrading: Applied affine transformations, random flips, rotations, and brightness adjustments to increase model resilience.
Handling Class Imbalance: Applied Random Over-Sampling to duplicate minority-class samples and achieve a more balanced representation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of data augmentation to improve generality and decrease overfitting.
Random shifts in width and height were applied.
Used during dynamic upgrading to increase model resilience.
Applied during dynamic upgrading to increase model resilience.
Used to handle class imbalance by duplicating minority-class samples (e.g., melanocytic nevi) to achieve a more balanced representation, rather than traditional geometric/color augmentation.

conclusions: The Vision Transformer (ViT) achieved the highest performance among all tested models, reaching a maximum accuracy of 99% (with specific metrics of 98% accuracy, 98.3% precision, 97.8% recall, and 98.0% F1-score on the HAM10000 dataset), outperforming VGG-16, ResNet-50, MobileNetV2, and a custom CNN baseline.

Transformer-based architectures offer superior feature representation and global contextual modeling compared to traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which often suffer from limited capacity for long-range dependencies and issues with overfitting or underfitting.
The ViT model demonstrated high predictive confidence in detecting melanomas, a critical factor for reducing false negatives and improving survival rates in clinical practice.
MobileNetV2 provided competitive results with significantly lower computational complexity, making it a viable option for lightweight or resource-constrained applications.

The ViT model exhibited strong generalization capabilities, maintaining a tight alignment between training and validation accuracy curves without significant divergence, unlike conventional CNNs which frequently show overfitting.

While CNN-based approaches remain competitive, their inherent architectural limitations prevent them from matching the generalization performance achieved by transformer-based models in this dermatological image analysis task.
Challenges such as class imbalance, computational efficiency, and model interpretability remain real issues when optimizing Vision Transformers for skin cancer datasets like HAM10000.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., heavily overrepresented melanocytic nevi) and heterogeneous imaging sources; while random over-sampling was used, this may not fully address distribution shifts in real-world clinical populations.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability beyond the specific HAM10000 distribution.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between the Vision Transformer and baseline models.

The reported high accuracy (98-99%) and confidence in melanoma detection require cautious interpretation as they are derived from a single dataset split (70/15/15) without cross-validation or external test sets.

The paper acknowledges that challenges such as model interpretability and computational efficiency remain real issues for deploying Vision Transformers in clinical settings, despite the strong classification results.

The study prioritizes overall classification accuracy and melanoma detection confidence but does not provide detailed per-class metrics (precision/recall/F1) for all seven classes, limiting the assessment of performance on rare or critical subtypes like dermatofibroma or vascular lesions.
